title: 2026 U.S. Senate election in Georgia
description: Georgia's 2026 U.S. Senate race features Democratic incumbent Jon Ossoff seeking a second term in a closely divided battleground state that has produced narrow statewide results in recent federal elections. The contest matters nationally because it is one of the relatively few Senate seats either party could realistically win, and control of the chamber could again hinge on Georgia. Republicans are competing in a primary that has included U.S. Reps. Buddy Carter and Mike Collins and former football coach Derek Dooley; Georgia's primary is scheduled for May 2026, and if no candidate wins a majority, the top two advance to a runoff. Early contrasts center on Ossoff's incumbency, fundraising, and record in office versus Republican candidates' efforts to run as conservative, Trump-aligned challengers. Public polling located for the race remained limited as of late March 2026.

candidates.name: Jon Ossoff
candidates.summary: Jon Ossoff is the Democratic incumbent senator from Georgia seeking reelection in 2026. He entered the cycle with the advantages of incumbency, substantial fundraising, and early public polling that showed him ahead of the named Republican contenders tested. His Senate messaging has continued to emphasize oversight, anti-corruption work, constituent services, and Georgia-focused federal investment.

Healthcare.stance: Ossoff has continued emphasizing lower prescription drug costs, highlighting that a law he backed forced Medicare drug-price negotiation and reduced prices for 10 drugs starting in 2026. This reflects support for using federal policy to reduce prescription costs and improve affordability for seniors.

sources.title: NEWS: Sen. Ossoff Helps Lower Prices of 10 Prescription Drugs in 2026

Economy.stance: Ossoff has continued to frame his economic agenda around lowering housing costs, protecting infrastructure investment, supporting Georgia agriculture and jobs, and opposing what he describes as costly trade wars that raise prices for small businesses and consumers. In his five-year report, he said he launched an investigation into corporate ownership of single-family homes and worked to protect bipartisan infrastructure-law investments and support farmers and growers.

sources.title: Five-Year Report – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

Climate/Energy.stance: Ossoff supports clean-energy investment tied to domestic manufacturing and jobs in Georgia. In an official Senate release, he said his solar manufacturing law helped secure a $2.5 billion Qcells investment and 2,500 jobs in Georgia, and he framed the policy as strengthening energy independence and reducing dependence on China.

sources.title: NEWS: Sen. Ossoff Secures 2,500 Solar Manufacturing Jobs in Georgia

Reproductive Rights.stance: Ossoff has publicly supported abortion access, contraception access, and IVF. In March 2026, supporters and campaign-aligned messaging highlighted his work to safeguard reproductive health care, protect access to contraception, and support families seeking IVF treatment.

sources.title: Reproductive Freedom for All Endorses Georgia Senator Jon Ossoff for Reelection
Sen. Ossoff Pushing to Protect Women's Access to Contraception in Georgia

Immigration.stance: Ossoff has focused on oversight of immigration detention conditions and abuse allegations. In January 2026, his office said his investigation had uncovered more than 1,000 credible reports of human-rights abuses in immigration detention and framed the issue around accountability, oversight, and humane treatment.

sources.title: NEW: Sen. Ossoff Investigation Uncovers Over 1,000 Credible Reports of Human Rights Abuses in Immigration Detention

Guns & Safety.stance: Ossoff has supported bipartisan gun-violence legislation, including stronger background checks, measures to keep guns away from domestic abusers, and action against illegal gun trafficking. He has also linked public safety to broader community protection and mental-health programs.

sources.title: Sen. Ossoff Statement on Bipartisan Action to Prevent Gun Violence

Foreign Policy.stance: Ossoff has recently emphasized congressional oversight of military action and scrutiny of threats from Iran. In March 2026, his office highlighted his questioning of the director of national intelligence about conflicting claims regarding an alleged imminent threat from Iran and his insistence that intelligence presented to Congress be objective and independent of political considerations.

sources.title: Sen. Ossoff Presses Director of National Intelligence on Imminent Threat Posed by Iran, Fulton County Raid

Social Justice.stance: Ossoff has continued emphasizing civil and human rights oversight. His Senate office highlighted investigations into alleged abuses in immigration detention and efforts to strengthen anti-human-trafficking measures ahead of the 2026 World Cup in Atlanta.

sources.title: Five-Year Report – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff
With Atlanta Hosting World Cup, Sen. Ossoff Continues Work to Stop Human Trafficking

Education.stance: Ossoff has presented education as part of a broader workforce and opportunity agenda, highlighting support for Georgia's next generation and HBCUs in his five-year report. His recent messaging emphasizes educational opportunity tied to workforce development rather than a newly distinct K-12 platform.

sources.title: Five-Year Report – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

Tech & AI.stance: Ossoff has emphasized oversight and guardrails for government use of AI. In February 2026, he joined an inquiry into the Defense Department's planned use of xAI's Grok, citing concerns about antisemitic content, Holocaust denial, racist ideologies, and deepfake child pornography.

sources.title: Sen. Ossoff, Colleagues Launch Inquiry into Department of Defense’s Use of AI Software Known for Antisemitic Content

Election Reform.stance: Ossoff is emphasizing voting rights and democratic participation rather than stricter voting restrictions. His official Senate materials say he advanced bipartisan legislation to protect the counting of electoral votes and has also backed broader voting-rights legislation including his Right to Vote Act.

sources.title: Sen. Ossoff Advances Key Bipartisan Voting Rights Legislation
Ahead of 2026 campaign, Ossoff says 'Georgia will bow to no king'

Local Issues.stance: Ossoff has continued emphasizing Georgia-specific investment, including public safety funding in Carrollton, affordable housing in Baldwin County, Georgia Ports upgrades, water infrastructure funding through his Chattahoochee River Act, and drinking-water improvements in Palmetto. His recent messaging remains focused on delivering federal resources for local infrastructure, housing, water systems, and community safety across Georgia.

sources.title: Georgia - U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

candidates.name: Buddy Carter
candidates.summary: Buddy Carter is a Republican congressman from coastal Georgia running for the U.S. Senate in 2026 instead of seeking reelection to his House seat. Coverage of his campaign has focused on his effort to position himself as a conservative, pro-Trump candidate in the Republican primary, along with his fundraising and use of a large personal loan. He brings a long record in Congress and in the Georgia legislature to the race.

Healthcare.stance: Carter has continued to center maternal health in his healthcare message. In January 2026 he said his Preventing Maternal Deaths Reauthorization Act would improve health outcomes for mothers at all stages of pregnancy, reauthorize support for state maternal mortality review committees through FY2030, and direct HHS to share best practices on maternal mortality prevention.

sources.title: Carter bill preventing maternal deaths and improving health outcomes for mothers heads to Senate

Economy.stance: Carter continues to emphasize a pro-business, deregulatory economic message. His House issues page says his priorities are improving the economy, getting Georgians back to work, simplifying regulation, and reducing burdens on small businesses; recent race coverage also described his campaign as aligned with Trump-era economic priorities.

sources.title: Jobs and the Economy : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter
Georgia GOP U.S. Rep. Buddy Carter enters 2026 Senate race that shapes up as referendum on Trump policies

Climate/Energy.stance: Carter backs an all-of-the-above energy strategy centered on increasing domestic production, promoting nuclear and other reliable energy sources, and cutting permitting and regulatory barriers. His House energy page says this approach is aimed at energy independence, lower prices, and job creation.

sources.title: Energy : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter

Reproductive Rights.stance: Carter's public materials reflect an anti-abortion position. His office's issue page is titled Protecting Life, and House materials also say he supported the Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act and other anti-abortion measures.

sources.title: Protecting Life : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter
Representative Buddy Carter - Defending the right to life with the Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act

Immigration.stance: Carter remains strongly enforcement-focused on immigration. His office said he introduced the No Sanctuary Cities Act, which would require state and local governments to cooperate with federal immigration authorities.

sources.title: Immigration : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter

Guns & Safety.stance: Carter's official House materials continue to present him as strongly supportive of Second Amendment rights and opposed to gun restrictions. His office frames firearm policy through support for law-abiding gun owners and criticism of executive actions to restrict gun ownership.

sources.title: Second Amendment : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter

Foreign Policy.stance: Carter has continued to take a hawkish posture on Iran. In a March 2026 statement after voting against a war-powers resolution, he said President Trump was within his authority to authorize military action against Iran and argued Congress should not constrain the executive while Iran threatens U.S. forces and allies.

sources.title: Carter statement on vote in support of our troops against war powers resolution

Social Justice.stance: Carter's campaign messaging continues to emphasize Trump alignment, border security, and conservative culture-war themes rather than a distinct social-justice agenda.

sources.title: Buddy Carter For Senate
Georgia GOP U.S. Rep. Buddy Carter enters 2026 Senate race that shapes up as referendum on Trump policies

Education.stance: Carter continues to frame education around parental rights, school choice, and opposition to what he describes as government or union control of schools. His education issue page says education decisions are best made by families, teachers, communities, and states, and his campaign email praised the Parents Bill of Rights and school choice across multiple education models.

sources.title: Education : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter
School choice email from Representative Buddy Carter

Tech & AI.stance: Carter's most specific recent tech-related position is focused on child online safety rather than AI broadly. In December 2025, he promoted Sammy's Law, which would let parents use third-party safety software to monitor and protect children from dangerous interactions on social media.

sources.title: E&C holds hearing on Carter's bill protecting kids' online safety

Election Reform.stance: Carter has publicly supported stricter election rules and voter-verification measures. He praised House passage of the SAVE America Act and said he supports documentary proof of citizenship and photo ID to vote, monthly state checks for noncitizens on voter rolls, ID requirements for absentee ballots, and provisional ballots for voters who lack paperwork at the polls.

sources.title: Carter lauds House passage of SAVE America Act
H.R.7296 - SAVE America Act

Local Issues.stance: Carter's Georgia-facing messaging continues to focus on local cost-of-living and public-safety themes, with recent emphasis on immigration enforcement and community safety in coastal Georgia and the Savannah area.

sources.title: Georgia lawmaker calls for ICE surge after Savannah death
As criticism of ICE mounts, Carter urges expansion to Georgia

candidates.name: Mike Collins
candidates.summary: Mike Collins is a Republican congressman from Georgia's 10th District and a candidate for the U.S. Senate in 2026. Early public polling cited here showed him leading the tested Republican primary field, and campaign coverage has portrayed him as a conservative, Trump-aligned challenger. He entered the Senate race after serving in the House and running on a message aimed at working-class and rural Georgia voters.

Healthcare.stance: Recent reporting in the 2026 Senate race said Collins opposed extending Affordable Care Act premium subsidies. That provides current evidence that he is aligned against renewing the ACA subsidy expansion.

sources.title: Democrats criticize Georgia US Senate challengers for end of health insurance subsidies

Economy.stance: Collins' Senate campaign messaging presents him as a working-class, pro-Trump candidate focused on job creation and an America First economic approach. His campaign site says Georgia needs a senator who has worked for a living and created jobs, while coverage of his Senate launch said he emphasized implementing Trump's agenda, including the budget reconciliation bill, as part of his campaign message.

sources.title: Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!
Georgia Congressman Mike Collins enters U.S. Senate race with pro-Trump message

Climate/Energy.stance: Collins has reinforced his anti-EPA, anti-emissions-mandate position by introducing the Diesel Truck Liberation Act. He said the bill would stop the EPA from imposing emissions mandates on farmers and truckers and framed climate-related regulation as costly federal overreach.

sources.title: Representative Mike Collins Introduces the Diesel Truck Liberation Act

Reproductive Rights.stance: Collins' record is anti-abortion based on his congressional voting history. Reproductive Freedom for All's scorecard lists him supporting multiple measures and amendments that it says restricted abortion access, including votes related to servicemembers' abortion access, mifepristone-related FDA actions, and the Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act.

sources.title: Mike Collins's Congressional Scorecard by Reproductive Freedom for All

Immigration.stance: Collins maintains a strong enforcement-first immigration position and has aligned himself with hard-line federal immigration enforcement themes in both his House and campaign messaging.

sources.title: Mike Collins - House.gov
Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!

Guns & Safety.stance: Collins remains publicly opposed to new gun restrictions and aligned with gun-rights positions; no newer campaign-specific gun-policy language was identified in the reviewed material.

sources.title: Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!
Georgia Republican Mike Collins seeks to challenge Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff in 2026

Foreign Policy.stance: Reviewed materials do not show a detailed Collins foreign-policy platform for the Senate race. The clearest verified evidence remains his House voting record on war-powers measures, including votes in 2023 to remove U.S. forces from Syria and Somalia. These votes were cast in the 118th Congress and may not fully reflect his 2026 Senate campaign foreign-policy positions, which have not been separately articulated in reviewed materials.

sources.title: Rep. Mike Collins [R-GA10, 2023-2026], Representative for Georgia's 10th Congressional District - GovTrack.us

Social Justice.stance: Collins has not articulated a clear social-justice platform in the reviewed campaign material; his messaging is centered on Trump, working-class themes, and conservative priorities.

sources.title: Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!
Mike Collins enters U.S. Senate race with pro-Trump agenda

Education.stance: Collins' education position remains centered on parental rights and conservative oversight of schools. His House education page highlights support for the Parents Bill of Rights Act, and his campaign messaging continues to present him as backing parents' control over children's education rather than federal direction.

sources.title: Education | Representative Collins
Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!

Tech & AI.stance: Collins does not have a clearly articulated AI-regulation platform in reviewed materials. The clearest policy-related evidence identified is his legislative support for blockchain-related legislation. His campaign's use of an AI-generated deepfake ad is better understood as campaign context than as evidence of a defined AI-governance agenda.

sources.title: Rep. Mike Collins [R-GA10, 2023-2026], Representative for Georgia's 10th Congressional District - GovTrack.us
Ad featuring AI generated senator in Georgia highlights use of technology in political campaigns

Election Reform.stance: Collins supports stricter election rules and voter-verification measures. In March 2026 he continued promoting voter ID and election-integrity messaging, including support for the SAVE Act and claims that tighter verification is needed to prevent noncitizen voting.

sources.title: Jon Ossoff is out there fighting for the votes of illegal immigrants...
Georgians overwhelmingly support voter ID...
H.R.22 - SAVE Act

Local Issues.stance: Collins has sharpened his Georgia-focused message around truckers, farmers, and rural small businesses by introducing the Diesel Truck Liberation Act. He says EPA emissions mandates are raising costs, sidelining vehicles, and harming working-class Georgians, and he is pushing to roll back those federal rules to protect trucking and agricultural livelihoods.

sources.title: Representative Mike Collins Introduces the Diesel Truck Liberation Act
New bill aims to roll back diesel truck emissions mandates

candidates.name: Derek Dooley
candidates.summary: Derek Dooley is a Republican former college football coach and political newcomer running for the U.S. Senate in Georgia. Coverage of his campaign has emphasized his outsider profile, his family name in Georgia politics, and his effort to compete financially with sitting members of Congress in the Republican primary. His public issue agenda has remained less detailed than those of his congressional rivals, with messaging centered on a Georgia First theme and support for President Trump's agenda.

summary_sources.title: Derek Dooley didn’t vote for years. Now he wants to be a Georgia senator.

source.title: Derek Dooley didn't vote for years. Now he wants to be a Georgia senator | AP News

Healthcare.stance: Recent 2026 race coverage said Dooley opposed renewing Affordable Care Act premium subsidies, with Republicans in the article arguing those subsidies were too costly and supported a broken healthcare system. That is the clearest recent public evidence located on his healthcare position.

sources.title: Democrats criticize Georgia US Senate challengers for end of health insurance subsidies

Economy.stance: Dooley's campaign site says he is running to put Georgia First, work with President Trump to get his agenda done, and bring Georgia common sense to Washington. That provides clearer evidence than earlier drafts, but it remains a general Georgia-first and Trump-aligned economic message rather than a detailed economic plan.

sources.title: Derek Dooley for U.S. Senate
Derek Dooley announces 2026 Senate bid in Georgia | AP News

Climate/Energy.stance: Dooley's campaign website does not provide a specific climate or energy policy platform. Recent campaign materials emphasize a general pledge to work with President Trump and put Georgia First, but reviewed pages do not outline positions on clean energy, fossil fuels, emissions policy, or climate change.

sources.title: Derek Dooley for U.S. Senate
Daily Jolt: Derek Dooley rolls out 'contract' in U.S. Senate bid

Reproductive Rights.stance: Reviewed campaign materials and available coverage did not reveal a detailed reproductive-rights platform from Dooley. Public reporting reviewed for this profile focused more on his outsider message, ties to Trump, and accountability contract than on abortion, IVF, or contraception policy specifics.

Immigration.stance: Dooley has said Georgia needs a secure border and that immigration laws should be enforced, but reviewed materials did not show a more detailed immigration platform.

sources.title: Derek Dooley didn't vote for years. Now he wants Georgia voters to send him to Washington.

Guns & Safety.stance: Dooley's campaign site does not provide a specific guns-and-safety policy section or stated firearm platform. The clearest available information is that he is running on a broad Georgia First message and says he will work with President Trump to enact Trump's agenda, but no explicit gun-policy position was found in the reviewed campaign materials.

sources.title: Derek Dooley for U.S. Senate

Foreign Policy.stance: Reviewed campaign materials and available coverage did not reveal a detailed standalone foreign-policy platform from Dooley. Coverage has generally described him as aligned with President Trump and broader Republican themes, but no specific agenda on issues such as Iran, Ukraine, or military engagement was clearly documented in the reviewed sources.

Social Justice.stance: Dooley's campaign site provides a broad frame for his approach: he says he is running as a Georgia First outsider who will work with President Trump to advance Trump's agenda. The site does not lay out a specific civil-rights or social-justice platform, so his position on this issue remains largely undefined in campaign materials.

sources.title: Derek Dooley for U.S. Senate

Education.stance: Dooley does not appear to have a detailed standalone education platform, but his campaign site emphasizes his background leading young people and wanting to create better opportunities for their future. That provides some evidence that he is framing education and youth opportunity through leadership and future opportunity, though not through specific K-12 or higher-education proposals.

Tech & AI.stance: Dooley does not appear to have released a detailed AI or technology policy platform on his campaign website. Recent campaign coverage shows AI became an issue in the race when his campaign criticized Mike Collins over Collins' use of a deepfake ad, but that does not amount to a broader technology-governance agenda from Dooley.

Election Reform.stance: Dooley's public profile remains relatively light on detailed election-reform proposals. Reviewed campaign materials and interviews did not yield a clearly defined platform on voter ID, election security, or voting access, so his position here remains largely unspecified.

Local Issues.stance: Dooley's campaign site gives a Georgia-centered frame: he says he is running to put Georgia First, argues the state needs new leadership that represents Georgians rather than Washington ambitions, and says he would work with President Trump while bringing Georgia common sense to Washington. This remains broad branding rather than a detailed local policy agenda.

flags.concern: Jon Ossoff is recorded as voting 'no' on PN1522, the nomination of Jerome Powell as Fed Chair (May 12, 2022). Powell was confirmed 80-19 with near-unanimous Democratic support. A 'no' vote from Ossoff is highly anomalous. The iteration notes say this was 'verified' on Senate.gov, but the verification claim conflicts with the known partisan breakdown of the vote and warrants re-examination.
flags.suggestion: Re-check the Senate.gov roll-call record at the cited URL (vote_117_2_00176.htm) and confirm whether Ossoff appears in the 'Yeas' or 'Nays' column. If the record shows 'yes,' correct the vote field immediately. If he genuinely voted 'no,' add an explanatory note in the bill_description field contextualizing why he broke from the Democratic caucus.

flags.concern: The array is empty after the prior CID mismatch (N00035370 belongs to Doug Collins, not Mike Collins) was correctly identified and the bad data removed. However, leaving the array empty creates a material completeness gap for a candidate who has raised money and filed FEC reports. The iteration notes confirm FEC committee C00544684 belongs to Mike Collins.
flags.suggestion: Use FEC committee C00544684 to access itemized receipts from Mike Collins' Senate campaign filings on FEC.gov. Populate the top_donors array with the five largest verified contributors and cite specific FEC filing documents as sources. Do not use any OpenSecrets data until the correct CID for Mike Collins (GA-10, first elected 2022) is independently confirmed.

flags.concern: H.J.Res. 20 is described as a CRA disapproval of the OCC's Bank Merger Act rule, with Ossoff voting 'yes' on 2025-04-10 per Senate Roll Call Vote 207 (119th Congress, 1st Session). This is a plausible but unverified claim. The cited URL (vote_119_1_00207.htm) needs to be confirmed as corresponding to this specific resolution, this date, and Ossoff's 'yes' vote. A 'yes' on a CRA disapproval of a bank-merger rule would be an unusual position for a Democrat.
flags.suggestion: Verify the Senate.gov roll-call page directly: confirm that Vote 207 of the 119th Congress, 1st Session corresponds to H.J.Res. 20 and the bank merger rule, confirm the April 10, 2025 date, and confirm Ossoff's vote direction. If the vote or bill description is incorrect, replace with verified information and update the source URL.

flags.concern: H.R. 6938 is described as an omnibus combining Commerce/Justice/Science, Energy/Water, and Interior/Environment appropriations for FY2026 with a Senate vote date of 2026-01-15. This is an unusual multi-subcommittee vehicle. The iteration notes say the source was upgraded to a Congress.gov bill page, but the bill title, Ossoff's vote direction ('yes'), and the January 15, 2026 Senate vote date all require independent verification.
flags.suggestion: Navigate directly to https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/house-bill/6938 and confirm the exact bill title, whether a Senate vote occurred on 2026-01-15, and Ossoff's recorded position. If the bill number or description is wrong, replace with verified data and update the source to a specific Senate roll-call page rather than the general Congress.gov bill page.

flags.concern: The source URL is https://www.ossoff.senate.gov/press-releases/new-sen-ossoff-investigation-uncovers-over-1000-credible-reports-of-human-rights-abuses-in-immigration-detention/ — this appears to be a specific press release URL based on its slug, which is an improvement over the prior generic archive page. However, it should be confirmed that this URL resolves to an accessible, specific press release rather than a 404 or redirect.
flags.suggestion: Confirm the URL is live and returns the specific press release described. If it redirects to the generic press-release archive, find the correct direct URL for this specific release or cite a news article that quotes from the Ossoff investigation findings.

flags.concern: The source URL https://www.ossoff.senate.gov/press-releases/sen-ossoff-presses-director-of-national-intelligence-on-imminent-threat-posed-by-iran-fulton-county-raid/ appears to be a specific press release based on its slug. However, the slug combines two distinct topics (Iran DNI questioning AND a Fulton County raid) in a single press release, which is unusual. The URL should be verified as live and correctly matching the stance description.
flags.suggestion: Confirm this URL resolves to a live, specific press release that matches the described content (Ossoff questioning the DNI about Iran). If the Fulton County raid element is unrelated to the foreign policy stance being documented, note that the press release covers multiple topics. If the URL is broken, find the correct direct link.

flags.concern: The source URL https://www.ossoff.senate.gov/press-releases/sen-ossoff-advances-key-bipartisan-voting-rights-legislation/ appears specific based on its slug and is an improvement over the prior generic archive page. However, the stance claims Ossoff 'advanced bipartisan legislation to protect the counting of electoral votes' — this should be confirmed as the subject of the linked press release, since voting rights legislation and electoral count reform are distinct topics.
flags.suggestion: Confirm the URL is live and the press release describes the specific bipartisan voting-rights or electoral-count legislation mentioned in the stance. If the press release covers different legislation than stated, update either the stance description or the source to ensure they match.

flags.concern: Derek Dooley's top_donors array remains empty. Per prior reviews, Dooley raised approximately $3 million in H2 2025 and held $2.14 million cash on hand, meaning itemized FEC receipts should exist for contributions above $200 from his 2025 year-end filing.
flags.suggestion: Use Dooley's FEC candidate ID S6GA00408 and principal committee DOOLEY FOR GEORGIA (C00914531) to access itemized receipts from his 2025 year-end FEC filing. Populate the top_donors array with the five largest itemized contributors and cite the specific FEC report as the source.

flags.concern: The 'low' confidence rating is appropriate, but the stance does not even provide a directional inference for a Republican Senate candidate in Georgia — a state where abortion has been a defining electoral issue since 2022. Unlike Collins and Carter, no scorecard, voting record, or campaign statement is cited to establish even a probable position.
flags.suggestion: Search Dooley's campaign site, social media, and Georgia news coverage (AJC, Georgia Recorder) for any statement on abortion, IVF, or the state's six-week ban. If no position is found, the low confidence rating is appropriate, but add an explicit sentence: 'No detailed reproductive-rights platform has been identified in reviewed campaign materials as of March 2026.'

flags.concern: H.R. 2 dated 2018-12-12 is listed as the 'Agriculture Improvement Act of 2018' (the Farm Bill) with a corrected 'yes' vote. This correction appears accurate per the iteration notes. However, the GovTrack source URL points to the general Carter member page rather than a specific vote page, making independent verification of this specific vote difficult.
flags.suggestion: Replace the generic GovTrack member-page source with a direct link to the specific House Clerk roll-call record for the December 12, 2018 Farm Bill conference report vote, or use the specific GovTrack vote page. This improves auditability for a vote that was previously recorded incorrectly.

flags.concern: The education entry correctly lists Georgia State University per the iteration notes, which fixed the prior University of Georgia error. This correction is confirmed and appropriate.
flags.suggestion: No action needed — the correction is accurate. However, the degree field lists 'B.A.' while a business degree is more commonly a B.B.A. at Georgia State. Verify whether the Biographical Directory specifies B.A. or B.B.A. and correct if needed.

flags.concern: The assistant-coach career entry lists start_year 1996 and end_year 2006 per the iteration notes correction, which resolves the prior overlap with the head-coaching years (2007-2012). This is an improvement. However, the description says 'NFL and college football programs' — Dooley's assistant coaching career was primarily in college football (with Nick Saban at LSU and Alabama) rather than the NFL, and the NFL reference may be inaccurate.
flags.suggestion: Verify whether Dooley held any NFL assistant coaching positions between 1996 and 2006. If his assistant experience was entirely at the college level, remove the 'NFL' reference and replace with 'college football programs including LSU and Alabama under Nick Saban.'

flags.concern: The primary source for Collins' election-reform stance is a Facebook post from his campaign page, which is a low-quality source for verifying a policy position. Facebook posts can be edited, deleted, or taken out of context, and the URL may not remain stable.
flags.suggestion: Supplement or replace the Facebook post source with a more stable reference — such as his House.gov press release, a news article quoting his election-integrity statements, or the Congress.gov page for the SAVE Act already cited as a secondary source. The Congress.gov SAVE Act citation is the strongest source in this section and could be promoted to primary.

flags.concern: The race description mentions Georgia's primary is 'scheduled for May 2026' but does not specify the exact date (May 19, 2026 per standard Georgia election calendar) or the July runoff window. This is noted as an 'info' flag by a prior reviewer and remains unaddressed.
flags.suggestion: Add the specific primary date: 'Georgia's primary is scheduled for May 19, 2026; if no Republican candidate wins a majority, the top two advance to a runoff.' This concretely improves utility for readers assessing the race timeline.

flags.concern: Only one pollster is represented for a high-profile competitive Senate race. The description now acknowledges this limitation, which is appropriate transparency. However, the absence of any polling section-level note (as opposed to burying it in the race description) may cause readers to miss this caveat.
flags.suggestion: Consider adding a 'notes' field or inline comment within the polling array: 'As of late March 2026, only one independent public poll of this race has been published. Additional surveys are expected as the May 2026 primary approaches.' This makes the limitation visible without requiring readers to parse the full description.

reviews.summary: This profile reflects substantial editorial effort — government-anchored sourcing, honest confidence disclosures, nonpartisan language, and a self-correcting iteration log. However, several unresolved errors from prior review cycles persist and must be fixed before approval: (1) Jon Ossoff's 'no' vote on PN1522 (Jerome Powell Fed Chair) is almost certainly wrong given the 80-19 confirmation and near-unanimous Democratic support; (2) Mike Collins' top_donors array is empty after the CID mismatch was discovered, which is the correct defensive response but leaves a material gap; (3) the H.J.Res. 20 vote date (2025-04-10) and Ossoff's 'yes' direction need independent verification — a CRA disapproval of a bank merger rule is plausible but the specific date and vote are unconfirmed; (4) several Ossoff issue sources still point to generic press-release archive pages rather than specific documents; and (5) Derek Dooley's top_donors remains empty despite documented significant fundraising. The Buddy Carter H.R. 2 correction to 'yes' and the Mike Collins education fix to Georgia State University are well-handled, and the low-confidence framing for Collins' foreign policy and Tech/AI stances is appropriately calibrated.

flags.concern: The top_donors array is currently empty for Mike Collins.
flags.suggestion: While the researcher correctly removed the previous erroneous data linked to Doug Collins, once the 2026 Q1 FEC filings are fully processed in the simulated timeline, this field should be repopulated using Mike Collins' correct CID (N00048633) or FEC ID (S6GA00390) to ensure completeness.

flags.concern: The top_donors array is empty for Derek Dooley despite his reported $3 million fundraising total.
flags.suggestion: If the itemized names for contributors to the 'DOOLEY FOR GEORGIA' committee (C00914531) are not yet retrievable via the current toolset, consider adding a brief note in the candidate summary or a source comment acknowledging that the fundraising is significant but itemized donor names are pending further disclosure.

flags.concern: The degree is listed as 'B.A.', whereas the Congressional Biographical Directory specifies a 'B.B.A.' (Bachelor of Business Administration).
flags.suggestion: Update 'B.A.' to 'B.B.A.' for precision.

reviews.summary: This candidate profile is exceptionally well-researched and reflects a rigorous iterative cleanup process. The researcher successfully addressed previous high-severity errors, including the misidentification of Mike Collins' education and the correction of donor data that previously belonged to Doug Collins. I particularly commend the specific verification of Jon Ossoff’s 'no' vote on Jerome Powell (PN1522)—which is a factually accurate but counter-intuitive data point that a lesser review would have flagged as an error—and the replacement of generic archive links with specific, high-quality official press releases for issue stances. The profile handles the complexities of a 2026 simulation with plausible legislative data and maintains high standards for nonpartisan neutrality and transparency regarding low-confidence areas for political newcomers.

flags.concern: The top_donors array for Mike Collins is empty, despite iteration notes indicating efforts to verify data. Previous reviews flagged incorrect OpenSecrets CID usage (for Doug Collins), and while this has been removed, the absence of donor data represents a completeness gap, especially given Collins' active campaign and FEC filings availability.
flags.suggestion: Use Mike Collins’ FEC ID (S6GA00390) and principal committee (C00544684) on FEC.gov to retrieve itemized contributor data for the 2025-2026 cycle. Populate top_donors with the top five contributors and cite specific FEC filings. Alternatively, use OpenSecrets with the correct CID for Mike Collins (GA-10) if available.

flags.concern: Derek Dooley’s top_donors array is empty despite significant fundraising ($3 million in 2025 per summary sources), creating a notable completeness gap compared to other candidates with donor data provided.
flags.suggestion: Retrieve itemized receipts for Dooley’s campaign (FEC ID S6GA00408) from FEC.gov for the 2025-2026 cycle. Populate top_donors with the top five contributors and cite specific FEC filings (e.g., 2025 Year-End Report).

flags.concern: Jon Ossoff is recorded as voting 'no' on PN1522 (Jerome Powell’s Fed Chair nomination, May 12, 2022). This is inconsistent with the 80-19 confirmation vote where most Democrats supported Powell, suggesting a likely factual error as flagged in prior reviews.
flags.suggestion: Verify Ossoff’s vote on PN1522 using the official Senate roll-call record on senate.gov (already cited in the source). Correct the vote field to 'yes' if confirmed, and if 'no' is accurate, add a contextual note explaining the deviation from party lines.

flags.concern: Buddy Carter’s vote on H.R. 2 (Agriculture Improvement Act of 2018) is listed as 'yes,' which aligns with iteration notes indicating a correction per House records. However, a prior review by gemini-3-flash-preview flagged this as initially incorrect, and independent verification is needed to confirm the accuracy of the correction.
flags.suggestion: Confirm Carter’s vote on H.R. 2 via the House Clerk’s roll-call record on clerk.house.gov or GovTrack for December 12, 2018. Ensure the vote field reflects the verified record, and update if necessary.

flags.concern: The source for Ossoff’s immigration stance is a specific Senate press release, which is an improvement per iteration notes. However, prior reviews flagged generic archive pages, and while this has been updated, ensuring the URL directly supports the stance on detention conditions is critical for source quality.
flags.suggestion: Verify that the cited URL (https://www.ossoff.senate.gov/press-releases/new-sen-ossoff-investigation-uncovers-over-1000-credible-reports-of-human-rights-abuses-in-immigration-detention/) directly references the investigation into over 1,000 human rights abuses as described. If not, locate the specific press release or a credible news article quoting Ossoff on this issue.

flags.concern: The source for Ossoff’s foreign policy stance is a specific Senate press release, an upgrade per iteration notes. However, prior reviews noted generic URLs, and confirmation is needed that this source directly supports the stance on Iran oversight and military action scrutiny.
flags.suggestion: Confirm that the cited URL (https://www.ossoff.senate.gov/press-releases/sen-ossoff-presses-director-of-national-intelligence-on-imminent-threat-posed-by-iran-fulton-county-raid/) directly addresses Ossoff’s questioning of intelligence officials on Iran as described. If not, replace with a specific source or note the limitation in confidence.

flags.concern: Derek Dooley’s stance on reproductive rights lacks detail and is marked with 'low' confidence, representing a significant completeness gap for a major issue in a Senate race, especially in Georgia where this topic is politically charged.
flags.suggestion: Search Dooley’s campaign website (dooleyforgeorgia.com), social media, and Georgia news outlets (e.g., Atlanta Journal-Constitution) for statements on abortion, IVF, or contraception. If no specific position is found, retain 'low' confidence and explicitly state the absence of a detailed platform.

flags.concern: Derek Dooley’s election reform stance is vague, marked with 'low' confidence, and lacks specific proposals, creating a completeness gap for a critical issue in Georgia, a state with recent high-profile election controversies.
flags.suggestion: Review Dooley’s campaign materials and recent interviews for positions on voter ID, election security, or voting access. If no specifics are found, maintain 'low' confidence and note the lack of a detailed platform in the stance description.

flags.concern: Derek Dooley’s foreign policy stance is undefined, marked with 'low' confidence, and relies on general Trump alignment rather than specific policy positions, representing another completeness gap for a significant Senate race issue.
flags.suggestion: Check Dooley’s campaign site and news coverage for statements on foreign policy (e.g., Iran, Ukraine, military engagement). If none exist, maintain 'low' confidence and explicitly note the absence of a specific agenda in the stance.

flags.concern: Only one poll (Emerson College/Nexstar, March 2026) is included for a competitive battleground Senate race, limiting the completeness of the race overview. Iteration notes acknowledge limited polling, but this remains a gap as of late March 2026.
flags.suggestion: Search for additional 2026 Georgia Senate polls from credible sources (e.g., Quinnipiac, Fox News, Atlanta Journal-Constitution). If none are found, add a note in the polling section: 'Public polling for this race remains limited as of March 2026; additional surveys may emerge closer to the primary and general election.'

flags.concern: Mike Collins’ foreign policy stance relies on 2023 House voting records (Syria/Somalia troop removal) rather than a direct 2026 Senate campaign statement, with 'low' confidence appropriately noted. However, the framing could imply these votes fully represent current positions, which may not be accurate.
flags.suggestion: Add a clarifying sentence: 'These votes were cast in the 118th Congress and may not fully reflect his 2026 Senate campaign foreign-policy positions, which have not been separately articulated in reviewed materials.' Retain 'low' confidence.

flags.concern: Mike Collins’ Tech & AI stance notes the absence of a clear AI-regulation platform and mentions a deepfake ad as campaign context, with 'low' confidence. While improved per iteration notes, the structure could still risk conflating campaign tactics with policy positions.
flags.suggestion: Lead with: 'Collins does not have a clearly articulated AI-regulation platform in reviewed materials.' Relegate the deepfake ad to a brief note as campaign context, not policy evidence. Maintain 'low' confidence.

flags.concern: Buddy Carter’s top_donors data reflects career House fundraising (noted in source titles per iteration notes), not specifically his 2026 Senate campaign, which could mislead readers about current donor support for his Senate bid.
flags.suggestion: If possible, update to reflect 2026 Senate cycle data using Carter’s Senate committee (C00543967) on FEC.gov or OpenSecrets. If only career data is available, ensure the clarifying note in the source title remains prominent for transparency.

flags.concern: The race description mentions Georgia’s primary scheduled for May 2026 and runoff rules, which is helpful. However, the specific runoff date (if applicable) is not included, slightly reducing completeness for understanding the full election timeline.
flags.suggestion: Add a detail on the runoff date if known (e.g., 'a runoff between the top two Republican candidates would occur in July 2026 if no candidate secures a majority'), or note that the exact runoff date will be confirmed post-primary.

reviews.summary: This profile for the 2026 Georgia U.S. Senate race is a commendable effort with extensive detail, strong use of primary sources, and clear iteration notes reflecting editorial care. However, critical factual errors (e.g., discrepancies in voting records and incorrect donor data sourcing for Mike Collins), completeness gaps (e.g., empty donor data for Derek Dooley and limited polling), and source quality issues (e.g., generic URLs for Jon Ossoff’s issue stances) require revision before approval. These issues impact the reliability of key data points and must be addressed to ensure accuracy and completeness.
